item.content:encoded:
⏱️ Prep Time: 40 minutes

🍳 Cook Time: 0 minutes

⏰ Total Time: N/A minutes

🍽️ Servings: 6

📊 Difficulty: Medium

🥘 Ingredients

  • 1 large napa cabbage (about 2 pounds), cut into quarters
  • 1/4 cup sea salt
  • 5 cups water
  • 1 tablespoon grated ginger
  • 4 cloves garlic, minced
  • 2 teaspoons sugar
  • 3 tablespoons Korean red pepper flakes (gochugaru)
  • 4 green onions, chopped
  • 1 small carrot, julienned
  • 1 daikon radish, julienned
  • 2 tablespoons fish sauce

👩‍🍳 Instructions

  1. Start by cutting the napa cabbage into quarters and then cut each quarter into bite-sized pieces. Place the cabbage in a large bowl and sprinkle the sea salt evenly over it. Pour the water over the cabbage and toss to combine thoroughly. This salting step is crucial because it draws out excess moisture from the cabbage, which helps it stay crunchy during fermentation. Let it sit for at least 2 hours, tossing every 30 minutes to ensure even salting. Avoid skipping this step or salting unevenly, as it can lead to mushy or unevenly fermented kimchi.
  2. While the cabbage salts, prepare the kimchi paste by mixing grated ginger, minced garlic, sugar, Korean red pepper flakes, and fish sauce in a bowl. Adjust the amount of gochugaru depending on your spice tolerance. This paste is the heart of your kimchi’s flavor, so taste it and tweak seasoning if needed. If you prefer a milder kimchi, reduce the pepper flakes or add a bit more sugar for balance. The fish sauce adds a savory depth, but you can substitute with soy sauce for a vegetarian version.
  3. After the cabbage has softened, rinse it thoroughly under cold water 2-3 times to remove excess salt. Drain well using a colander and gently squeeze out any extra water. This rinsing step prevents your kimchi from being too salty and keeps the flavors balanced. Be careful not to press too hard or the cabbage will lose its crunch. Pat dry with a clean kitchen towel or paper towels to remove surface moisture.
  4. In a large mixing bowl, combine the drained cabbage with the prepared kimchi paste. Add the chopped green onions, julienned carrot, and daikon radish. Using your hands (wear gloves to avoid staining and irritation), massage the paste into the vegetables until everything is evenly coated. This thorough mixing ensures each bite has balanced flavor and helps activate the fermentation process. Don’t rush this step; take your time to blend the ingredients well.
  5. Transfer the kimchi mixture into a clean glass jar or airtight container, pressing down firmly to remove air pockets. Leave about an inch of headspace at the top to allow gases to expand during fermentation. Seal the jar tightly but not overly tight, as some air release is normal. This packing step is important to prevent mold growth and ensure anaerobic fermentation, which produces the signature tangy flavor.
  6. Leave the jar at room temperature, ideally between 65-75°F, for 1 to 2 days to start fermenting. You’ll notice bubbles forming and a sour aroma developing. Check daily and press kimchi down if it rises above the liquid. The timing depends on your room temperature and personal taste preference; warmer rooms speed up fermentation. Avoid opening the jar too often as it lets air in and can slow fermentation.
  7. After initial fermentation, move the kimchi to the refrigerator to slow down the process. It will continue to develop flavor but at a much slower pace. Refrigeration also helps preserve texture and freshness. You can start tasting it after 3 days, but it’s best after about a week when flavors fully mature. If you prefer a milder taste, consume sooner; for stronger tang, wait longer.
  8. When serving, use clean utensils to avoid contaminating the jar and extend shelf life. Kimchi pairs wonderfully with rice, soups, grilled meats, or even as a topping on tacos and burgers. If you want to add a fresh crunch, mix in some freshly chopped green onions or sesame seeds just before serving.
  9. If you want to store kimchi long-term, keep it refrigerated and consume within 3 months for best flavor and texture. Over time, it will become more sour and soft, which is still great for cooking but less ideal for fresh eating. Always check for off smells or mold before use. Properly fermented and stored kimchi is safe and delicious.

item.title: Smoky and Tender Pellet Grilled Chicken with Wood Pellets
item.description: This pellet grilled chicken is juicy and smoky without any hassle. Using wood pellets gives it that rich, deep flavor you just can't replicate. Perfect for weekend cookouts or relaxed family dinners! | ⏱️ Prep: 15min | Cook: 30min | Serves: 4 | #cat1761229889885 #Easypost #1hourmeals #post #cooking
item.dc:creator: author_001
item.content:encoded:
⏱️ Prep Time: 15 minutes

🍳 Cook Time: 30 minutes

⏰ Total Time: N/A minutes

🍽️ Servings: 4

📊 Difficulty: Easy

🥘 Ingredients

  • 4 boneless, skinless chicken breasts, trimmed
  • 2 tablespoons olive oil
  • 1 tablespoon smoked paprika
  • 1 teaspoon garlic powder
  • 1 teaspoon onion powder
  • 1 teaspoon salt
  • 1/2 teaspoon black pepper
  • Wood pellets (hickory or apple) for the pellet grill

👩‍🍳 Instructions

  1. Start by preheating your pellet grill to about 225°F. This lower temperature lets the chicken cook slowly and soak up the wood smoke. Make sure your grill is clean and the wood pellets are fresh for the best flavor. Avoid rushing this step because consistent heat is key for tender, juicy chicken instead of drying it out.
  2. While the grill warms, pat the chicken breasts dry with paper towels. This helps the seasoning stick and promotes better browning. Rub each breast with olive oil evenly to keep moisture locked in during cooking.
  3. Mix the smoked paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, salt, and black pepper in a small bowl. Sprinkle this seasoning blend evenly over both sides of each chicken breast. The smoked paprika is key here for that subtle smoky depth that complements the pellet grill’s flavor.
  4. Place the chicken breasts on the grill grates, spacing them out to ensure even smoke exposure. Close the lid and let them cook undisturbed at 225°F. Resist the urge to flip too often; slow and steady is the way to go for tender results.
  5. After about 20 minutes, check the internal temperature with a meat thermometer. Aim for 160°F before the final sear. If it hasn’t reached that, close the lid and let it cook a bit longer. This step ensures the chicken is safe to eat but still juicy.
  6. Increase the grill temperature to 400°F for a quick sear. Flip the chicken breasts and sear each side for 2-3 minutes to develop a nice caramelized crust. This final step adds texture and enhances flavor complexity.
  7. Remove the chicken from the grill and let it rest for 5 minutes. Resting lets the juices redistribute inside the meat, so it stays moist when you cut into it. Don’t skip this or you’ll lose all that delicious moisture.
  8. Slice the chicken against the grain for the best tenderness. Serve it with your favorite sides like grilled veggies or a fresh salad. The smoky flavor pairs beautifully with simple, bright sides.
  9. Clean your grill grates while still warm to prevent residue buildup and keep your pellet grill ready for next time. This quick maintenance step helps maintain flavor purity and extends grill life.
